sentences.text: He saved up much more too.
No, no.
I have to tell you before Los Angeles.
Because first, my husband started to work.
He saved up-- we saved up the first few dollars.
Then we-- then we borrowed some money.
And my sister and her second husband, and my husband and I, we went into a luncheon at Second Avenue and 10th Street in New York, Manhattan.
And by the way, my sister and I, we made it our business to start learning English.
I read comic books.
I read children's books.
And [INAUDIBLE] said, yeah, I know you know more languages, but English is important.
Thank God.
God rest her soul.
And we learned English in no time.
And when we were in the-- in the luncheonette, and we had a lot of very intelligent people coming in-- lawyers, and judges, and teachers, and all-- and I used to learn from them.
And then I started to make jokes in English, which was my-- my kind of thing.
And then-- and then if I learned a new word, I used to ask, what does it mean?
And if they said that's a bad word, I said, well, what does it mean?
Because if it's bad, I want to know why I shouldn't use it.
And we worked there until '53.
We-- they visited a farm, and they liked the life on farm, because we worked very hard in the luncheonette.
And we moved to Vineland, New Jersey, on the farm, which was a hard life, very hard.
I used to go to work and supported the chicken's feet.
[LAUGHTER] That's the truth.
That's why-- I worked so hard that that's the time I lost a baby.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- we were on the farm till '66, I think.
There I go with dates.
So we sold the farm, and we moved to Ventnor, which is at the outskirts of Atlantic City.
My-- and I kept-- oh, while I-- I worked in different places, factories, and I wound up working the last one in Vineland at the boutique for ladies' wear.
So when I came to Ventnor, my husband was driving a small-- it's a jitney.
It's a mini bus.
And I went to work in-- in the, again, to a boutique.
That's where I was working.
To California, I came-- my son came to California to visit some friends, a friend that was together with in-- in-- in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] college.
They went to Drexel in Philadelphia, if you ever heard about one of the top schools.
And he told them a lot of words he also used to say.
Your mother looks zaftig.
Well, anyhow, he-- so he moved here because his father was running a certain company, and he came here to visit.
And he liked the climate.
And Sam's father said, Marty, if you want to move here-- because he-- during-- may I go back a little bit?
Marty went-- was going to college, so they used to have six months school, six months work.
So one time there, some people were interviewed from different places.
So he was interviewed from Ford Motor Company.
And he was accepted to work for [INAUDIBLE] for the six months work.
And he worked at the-- at the-- near-- oh, God, I can think of the city.
No.
No.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyhow, he worked for them every six months that he-- but then I said, this next six months, I said, Marty, why don't you tell them that you would like to work closer, because there was one in New Jersey, also.
He said, Mom, you don't ask Ford where to work.
I said, they can't-- they won't slap you.
So sure enough, they did, because they-- when he worked the first six months, and they gave him a farewell party, all the people from the offices.
And he still has the-- the-- the-- a radio combination with the lamp, and a card was signed by the managers, by the secretaries, and everything.
That's how much they liked him.
So anyhow, when he applied, and they gave him the job to work in New Jersey, so he was closer to home.
So when he was out here in California, Sam's father said, well, if you like to move out here, I'll make out the job for you.
That is, you know, it's the kind of job that you should have.
And that's what he did.
So every time we used to come visit, I liked the climate.
And my son said, why you have to live there in the cold?
He knew I don't like the cold.
And all the telephone calls, because we called each other so many times a week.
So we moved here.
Have you had a--
And when I moved, my sister moved, because we all move with-- together.
And she lives here within walking distance, also.
Maybe the upbringing that we had, a very strong upbringing.
First of all, we had an upbringing is, it's better to give than to receive.
And also, before you take a bite in your mouth, you give to tsedakah, which means--
Charity.
Also, my mom always used to say, before you say something, chew on it for 10-- count to 10.
Because while you have it in you, it belongs to you.
Once you let go of it, it doesn't belong to you.
And it's the same thing with doing something.
So I had very smart parents, and the upbringing was out of this world.
Yes.
Five years ago this month, I was declared with cancer.
I beat it, just like I beat Hitler.
I'm here to talk about it.
And-- and my sense of humor kept me going, because I have-- if I say so myself, I have a marvelous sense of humor.
I make jokes about myself.
And I didn't despair, and I use myself as a psychologist.
I talk to myself.
I pay myself.
And I say, Helen, do you want to wind up on-- on drugs, or you want to wind up in a nut house?
And Helen says, no, I don't.
So there I cheer up, and I cheer up other people.
What about your participation in the Jewish community?
I participate as much as possible in our synagogue.
I belong to the neighborhood group.
I belong to the Hadassah Group.
B'nai Israel.
Congregation B'nai Israel.
I'm very happy with it.
When I joined it, we had a storefront, but I liked the people.
And I'm never-- I was never sorry that I joined that synagogue.
I love the people, and I'm more with young people than with old people.
[LAUGHS] Maybe that keeps me young.
Well, my husband-- we are married 49 years.
In May, we'll be 50.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And sorry to say that I have to watch over him a lot now, because I cannot go away.
His sister used to send me money to come.
She insisted to send me money for the fare, for me to come and visit with her for three weeks at a time when I moved out here.
But I can't do that anymore.
My sister-in-law that is in Las Vegas, I used to go out for a few days with my sister there.
That's gone.
I cannot do it because my husband is getting sick.
Not physically.
So I'm trying to be as best I can.
Yes.
The same, Poland.
No, [INAUDIBLE], Pietrokov.
I-- he was telling me all kinds of camps.
The only one that I  really know is-- [SIGHS] God-- Mauthausen and-- and-- and-- what-- I don't know.
I don't know.
Now, I'm losing my mind, also.
Because--
No, he can't.
He wouldn't, because--
His sister, the one that lives in New York that we are very, very, very close, and a brother that lives in New York.
One, two, three.
This is my husband of 49 years.
His name is Leo.
And next May, we will be married 50 years.
I deserve a medal, by the way.
This is my son.
[LAUGHTER] This is my son, Marty.
And this is my--
Grandson.
My grandson, also, my pride and joy, Arthur.
Oh, in several ways.
I would say mostly in that it's made me more sensitive to similar type problems going on in the world today.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Oftentimes at work we have a lot of discussions about-- about the Holocaust and how it relates to the world today.
A lot of people who aren't Jewish, or have not been involved in World War II, or not-- don't know survivors say, well, it happened then, but it can't happen again.
And of course, that kind of hits my hot button, because my feeling is, well, it's happening every day.
It's just that people aren't sensitive to it.
So it's just a heightened sensitivity.
Yeah, quite a bit.
It's difficult.
I mean, it's not a nice story.
I hope I've been successful in conveying that sensitivity, being aware of what's going on in the world today.
I hope.
No, because I have plenty of Jewish friends and plenty of non-Jewish friends and they don't have a problem with it at all.
I have a lot of Jewish friends and a Jewish youth group, you know.
Basically, that's it.
The Jewish youth group's about the big-- your major involvement.
Yeah.
That's the major involvement right there.
I mean, you don't get much other interaction.
I mean, a lot of times you go to school, you don't know someone's Jewish.
But I mean, in USY, you know, everyone's Jewish.
[SIGHS] Let me tell you something about my son and my grandson.
Well, the little one is not here.
But all I can tell you is in all what I went through, through the war, and since the liberation and all, these are the blessings that I thank God a hundred times a day.
My son was the easiest to raise.
Thank God had never problems with him.
And it's not just because I say so now.
But he was-- my neighbors and my friends always used to say, when there was-- they started to complain about their children, [LAUGHS] they used-- they used to say, you don't talk.
You don't have anything what to say.
[NON-ENGLISH].
He was.
He was [NON-ENGLISH].
He still is [NON-ENGLISH].
I'll tell you something else.
He is--
He is-- well, I'll get to him.
He is the son and daughter-- the daughter that I almost had.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Thank God.
This kid?
This one?
Yeah.
This kid is, when we go someplace out, and he'll hold onto his zaide.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] And when I showed him one time a picture of somebody that I met in-- in Berlin, that we-- his uncle was a cantor, and he wanted me very badly to date him.
Mm-hm.
Thank you.
Thank you.
You were a--
Thank you very much.
Thank you.
And you are really an inspiration.
I'll be honest to you.
It's not because you were a [INAUDIBLE], but you're a nice person.
And those guys are nice.
This picture is of my mother and father.
This is an enlargement from an original picture that was sent 1938 to the United States.
And I made an enlargement.
Her name is Devora Leah, and my father's name, Mattis Shlomo.
Last name Fromer.
In Warsaw.
Warsaw, Poland.
I'm sorry.
That's my mom and dad.
I was in some kind of a-- I was hurt sometime there, and they wanted to make me feel better.
And they took a picture with me.
That's my mom and my father.
And that's myself.
This is Devora Leah Fromer, Mattis Shlomo Fromer, and [?
Nahama ?] Fromer.
Approximately either-- [INAUDIBLE]
In Warsaw, Poland.
[INAUDIBLE].
I'm so anxious to talk
Well, I don't know if you can get the 25 in the flower bouquet.
That was the silver anniversary of my parents, Devor-- Devora Leah Fromer, Mattis Shlomo Fromer.
It was a huge party for their 25th wedding anniversary.
Not-- not-- not children.
They were grownups.
One is my brother, Leo Fromer.
He is the one that was in the United States.
The other one is just friends.
Oh my God, certainly.
I was a young school kid when that was taken.
And that was all Warsaw, Poland.
OK?
My brother.
That was not his profession, being-- he was amateur photographer.
So he took a picture of himself and my mom.
And [?
Edic ?] Fromer, mom, Devora Leah Fromer.
He put the camera on a timer, and they're sitting in front of a mirror, if you can see that-- the reflection in the mirror of the two of them.
Oh, that-- in the-- in the '30s.
Exactly, I couldn't pinpoint.
Everything happened in the '30s, in the Warsaw, because I can't pinpoint.
Till '39, everything was normal.
There's my mom.
Right in the corner of the picture.
Devora Leah Fromer.
Just to get together.
In my-- in our home.
This is a picture of my youngest brother and his wife.
This--
On the wall.
Oh, I have to use--
This is my, well--
This one.
Yeah.
Yannick. ?] I don't remember last names.
Not Jewish.
He spoke a better Yiddish than I did.
The youngest brother.
The one-- [?
Edic. ?]
In our home, yeah.
And this is my dad.
Mattis Shlomo
You took-- I don't want any.
And this one, next door neighbors.
And this is my sister Rosie, so I must be-- oh, and I'm here.
I remember them all, but--
I'll tell you something.
Yeah, OK.
The main thing is the family pic.
Time you get done with her, you'll learn Jewish.
I-- June-- June 7 was my bir-- my 16th birthday.
At that time, we never bought ready clothes, by the way.
Everything was made to order.
So I got my first grownup outfit with a little head of the face.
And-- and this is my-- the 16th birthday on a side street that my brother took.
This my brother took, that picture.
In Warsaw, on a side street.
Yeah, figure out.
No, yes.
Yeah, June 7, 1939.
Look at that.
What happened to me?
Oh, God.
[LAUGHTER]
OK.
I saw this picture, or-- or whatever you call, during a 1978, '79 when they were showing from Holocaust, a movie on television.
I saw my mom's-- I recognized my mother there.
And I went to New York to find the-- the-- the-- NBC-- NBC borrowed those pictures that the Nazis took.
And-- and there are three younger women, and the one that is older, you can see.
It-- she survived with-- she's surrounded-- excuse me-- she's surrounded with dead bodies and soldiers.
And they were the next to be exterminated.
[SIGHS] Who knows how long they were schlepping her around?
When she was taken-- when she was taken away.
So maybe they got her into the 60s, or whatever.
No.
I-- I don't know.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because you didn't wind up in your own cities, or your own places of birth.
This is my husband and myself, Leo Greenbaum, Helen Greenbaum, and in Berlin, Germany.
Well, my-- well, figure out.
My-- my-- my son was three-- three months in the tummy.
This is my son, Martin Greenbaum, my grandson, Arthur Greenbaum, and my younger grandson, Ian Greenbaurm.
[INAUDIBLE]
In Cali-- what?
Right here.
Right here in Irvine.
Yes.
One is 18, just turned 18.
And another one is 12.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- and-- and I brought--
And I brought this one that it survived in Czechoslovakia.
And I brought this, and that also survived.
No.
It was--
Well, my sister and I, Rosalie Wattenberg, and Helen Greenbaum, myself, are showing a table cloth that survived the war in-- in the United States, because my mother used to embroider.
That was her hobby.
And she sent this table cloth to my Aunt, [?
Rae. ?] And there are two initials on it, my-- my mom's initial and my Aunt's [?
Rea's ?] initial.
And I cherish that table cloth.
Hopefully, my-- my son will cherish it as much one day when he's going to get it.
He's going to get it.
Somebody made-- this was not a bought drawing.
Somebody did draw the-- my-- my-- about the-- a wish for Shabbat.
And-- and they made a herring, and a fish, and a bottle of wine, and a challah and my mom's name, Devora Leah Fromer, embroidered there.
And that was sent to the United States, also, to my-- to my Aunt [?
Rae. ?]
All before the war.
From Warsaw to New York.
